notes.section_title: Introduction: Understanding Vertical Division of Power

content.text: In the previous chapter, we noted that vertical division of power among different levels of government is one of the major forms of power-sharing in modern democracies. This form of power-sharing is most commonly referred to as federalism.

Belgium and Sri Lanka provide contrasting examples. Belgium shifted from a unitary to a federal form of government in 1993, where regional governments were given constitutional powers and were no longer dependent on the central government. In contrast, Sri Lanka continues to be a unitary system where the national government has all the powers, leading to demands for a federal system by Tamil leaders.

Federalism is a system of government in which the power is divided between a central authority and various constituent units of the country. Usually, a federation has two levels of government: one for the entire country and others at the level of provinces or states.

content.title: Dual Objectives of Federalism
content.text: A federal system has two main objectives: (1) to safeguard and promote the unity of the country, and (2) at the same time accommodate regional diversity.
Under the unitary system, either there is only one level of government or the sub-units are subordinate to the central government. The central government can pass on orders to the local governments.

In a federal system, the central government cannot order the state government to do something. The state government has powers of its own for which it is not answerable to the central government. Both are separately answerable to the people.

content.title: The Seven Pillars of Federalism
items: Two or more levels (tiers) of government.
Different tiers govern the same citizens, but each tier has its own jurisdiction in specific matters of legislation, taxation, and administration.
Jurisdictions are specified in the constitution; the authority of each tier is constitutionally guaranteed.
Fundamental provisions of the constitution cannot be unilaterally changed by one level of government; consent of both levels is required.
Courts (especially the Supreme Court) act as an umpire to interpret the constitution and resolve disputes between different levels of government.
Sources of revenue for each level are clearly specified to ensure financial autonomy.
Dual objectives: Safeguard unity while accommodating regional diversity.

content.text: The exact balance of power between the central and state governments varies from one federation to another, depending on the historical context of its formation.

rows: Independent States come together on their own to form a bigger unit, pooling sovereignty and retaining identity to increase security.
A large country decides to divide its power between the constituent States and the national government.

content.title: Power Dynamics
content.text: In 'Coming Together' federations, all constituent states usually have equal powers. In 'Holding Together' federations, the Central Government tends to be more powerful, and different constituent units often have unequal powers (some units get special powers).

notes.section_title: What Makes India a Federal Country?

content.text: The Constitution declared India as a 'Union of States'. Although it does not use the word federation, the Indian Union is based on the principles of federalism. Originally a two-tier system, a third tier of federalism was later added in the form of Panchayats and Municipalities.

content.title: The Three Lists
items: Union List: Subjects of national importance like Defence, Foreign Affairs, Banking, Communications, and Currency. Only the Union Government can make laws.
State List: Subjects of State and local importance like Police, Trade, Commerce, Agriculture, and Irrigation. Only State Governments can make laws.
Concurrent List: Subjects of common interest like Education, Forest, Trade Unions, Marriage, Adoption, and Succession. Both can make laws, but the Union law prevails in case of conflict.

content.title: Residuary Subjects
content.text: Subjects like computer software that came up after the constitution was made are called 'residuary' subjects. The Union Government has the power to legislate on these.

Not all States in the Indian Union have identical powers. Some States like Assam, Nagaland, Arunachal Pradesh, and Mizoram enjoy special status under Article 371 due to their peculiar social and historical circumstances.

Some units are too small to become independent States but could not be merged with existing States. These are called Union Territories (e.g., Chandigarh, Lakshadweep, Delhi). They do not have the powers of a State, and the Central Government has special powers in running them.

Sharing of power is basic to the structure of the Constitution. The Parliament cannot unilaterally change this arrangement. Any change requires approval by both Houses with at least two-thirds majority, followed by ratification by the legislatures of at least half of the total States.

content.title: Role of Judiciary
content.text: The judiciary oversees the implementation of constitutional provisions. In case of disputes over the division of powers, High Courts and the Supreme Court make the final decision.

The creation of linguistic States was the first and a major test for democratic politics in India. Boundaries of several old States were changed to ensure people speaking the same language lived in the same State (e.g., Nagaland, Uttarakhand, Jharkhand were based on culture/ethnicity).

content.title: Impact
content.text: While feared to lead to disintegration, the formation of linguistic States has actually made the country more united and administration easier.

The Constitution did not give the status of national language to any one language. Hindi was identified as the official language (mother tongue of 44% Indians), but safeguards were provided for other languages.

content.title: Linguistic Safeguards
items: 22 other languages are recognized as Scheduled Languages.
Candidates can take central government exams in any of these languages.
States have their own official languages.
Use of English for official purposes, which was to stop in 1965, continues alongside Hindi to accommodate non-Hindi speaking States (e.g., Tamil Nadu).

content.text: Restructuring Centre-State relations has strengthened federalism in practice. Earlier, single-party dominance often meant the Centre undermined State autonomy.

content.title: The Era of Coalition Governments
content.text: Post-1990, the rise of regional parties led to coalition governments at the Centre. This fostered a new culture of power sharing and respect for State autonomy, supported by a Supreme Court judgment making it harder for the Centre to arbitrarily dismiss State governments.

Decentralisation is when power is taken away from Central and State governments and given to local government. Large states like UP (bigger than Russia) or Maharashtra (as big as Germany) need power sharing within them due to their internal diversity.

content.title: Rationale
content.text: Local people have better knowledge of problems in their localities and better ideas on where to spend money and manage things efficiently. It also encourages direct democratic participation.

content.title: Key Provisions for Local Self-Government
items: Mandatory regular elections to local bodies.
Reservation of seats for SC, ST, and OBC.
One-third reservation for women in all positions.
Creation of an independent State Election Commission.
Mandatory sharing of powers and revenue by State governments with local bodies.

content.text: The local government structure exists for both rural and urban areas.

content.title: Gram Sabha
content.text: The Gram Sabha is the supervisory body for the Gram Panchayat, consisting of all voters in the village. It meets twice or thrice a year to approve budgets and review performance.

glossary.definition: A system of government in which power is divided between a central authority and various constituent units of the country.
The area over which someone has legal authority, defined in terms of geographical boundaries or specific subjects.
A system where either there is only one level of government or sub-units are subordinate to the central government.
A federation formed by independent states pooling their sovereignty to form a bigger unit while retaining their identity (e.g., USA).
A federation where a large country decides to divide its power between constituent states and the national government (e.g., India).
Subjects that do not fall in any of the three legislative lists and came up after the constitution was made.
The transfer of power from central and state governments to local government bodies.
A government formed by the coming together of at least two political parties, usually through an alliance.
The 22 languages included in the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution.

summary_points: Federalism involves the division of power between a central authority and constituent units, aimed at safeguarding unity while accommodating diversity.
The Indian Constitution provides a three-fold distribution of legislative powers through the Union, State, and Concurrent lists.
India is a 'holding together' federation where the Central government is relatively more powerful and some states enjoy special status.
Success of federalism in India is attributed to democratic politics, which fostered respect for diversity and a shared desire for living together.
Linguistic states and a flexible language policy have strengthened national unity rather than weakening it.
The 1992 Constitutional amendment revitalized local self-government (Panchayati Raj and Municipalities), making the third tier of democracy more effective.
While decentralisation has deepened democracy, challenges like irregular Gram Sabhas and limited transfer of resources remain.
